evidence.finding: (E)-cinnamaldehyde at 0.051 mg/cm2 provided 87% protection 30 minutes after application; DEET at 0.025 mg/cm2 provided 95% in the same study.
limitations: This is a dose- and time-specific laboratory skin result, not proof of longer protection, safety, or field performance.

supporting_provenance.title: Repellency of Cinnamomum cassia bark compounds and cream containing cassia oil to Aedes aegypti under laboratory and indoor conditions
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: skin bioassay protection at 30 minutes for (E)-cinnamaldehyde and DEET

evidence.finding: Cinnamaldehyde showed an 82% repellent rate in screening, and its nanoemulsion prolonged protection time relative to the normal solution in an arm-in-cage assay.
limitations: The formulation changed persistence; the result cannot be transferred unchanged to a different formulation or to Aedes aegypti.

supporting_provenance.title: Screening of larvicidal activity of 53 essential oils and their synergistic effect for the improvement of deltamethrin efficacy against Aedes albopictus
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: repellent screening and arm-in-cage nanoemulsion comparison

evidence.finding: Citral showed a 65% repellent rate in screening, and its nanoemulsion prolonged protection time relative to the normal solution in an arm-in-cage assay.
limitations: The formulation changed persistence; the result cannot be transferred unchanged to a different formulation or to Aedes aegypti.

supporting_provenance.title: Screening of larvicidal activity of 53 essential oils and their synergistic effect for the improvement of deltamethrin efficacy against Aedes albopictus
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: repellent screening and arm-in-cage nanoemulsion comparison

evidence.finding: At 20% in ethanol, Eucalyptus globulus oil prevented Aedes aegypti landing for about 1.5 hours in the reported protocol.
limitations: The result is specific to Eucalyptus globulus oil at 20%; it does not establish the composition or performance of another eucalyptus species, brand, or formulation.

supporting_provenance.title: Mosquito repellent activity of volatile oils from selected aromatic plants
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: 20% Eucalyptus globulus oil Aedes aegypti landing result

evidence.endpoint: movement away from treated filter paper
evidence.finding: Eugenol produced significant close-range repellency in Aedes aegypti in the 30-second odor assay.
limitations: The assay did not measure human-host landing, biting, complete protection time, or field efficacy.

supporting_provenance.title: Insect repellents mediate species-specific olfactory behaviours in mosquitoes
supporting_provenance.locator: Methods: close-proximity odor assay; Results: Aedes aegypti species comparison

evidence.endpoint: movement away from treated filter paper
evidence.finding: Eugenol produced a weak repellent response in the Anopheles coluzzii assay that was not significantly different from paraffin oil (P=0.08).
limitations: A non-significant result in this short close-range assay is not proof of no effect at every dose, formulation, or endpoint.

supporting_provenance.title: Insect repellents mediate species-specific olfactory behaviours in mosquitoes
supporting_provenance.locator: Methods: close-proximity odor assay; Results: Anopheles coluzzii species comparison and P=0.08

evidence.endpoint: population suppression after ingesting toxic bait
evidence.finding: Microencapsulated garlic oil was tested as the toxic active ingredient in an attractive sugar bait and reduced Aedes albopictus abundance.
limitations: This is pesticidal bait evidence, not spatial or topical repellency, and should not be presented as a repellent result.

supporting_provenance.title: Efficacy of attractive toxic sugar baits against Aedes albopictus with garlic oil encapsulated in beta-cyclodextrin as the active ingredient
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: garlic-oil attractive toxic sugar bait field treatment and population endpoint

evidence.finding: Geraniol showed moderate repellency to adult Drosophila suzukii in the laboratory screen.
limitations: The study ranked thymol above geraniol and did not establish geraniol field persistence or crop protection as a standalone compound.

supporting_provenance.title: Reduced Drosophila suzukii Infestation in Berries Using Deterrent Compounds and Laminate Polymer Flakes
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract and Results: laboratory repellency and oviposition-deterrence screening

evidence.finding: Geraniol was a 5% component of Ecotrol PLUS, a rosemary/geraniol/peppermint mixture that reduced raspberry sentinel-fruit infestation versus an unsprayed control.
limitations: The mixture study did not isolate geraniol's contribution and did not establish blueberry efficacy or equivalence to spinosad.

supporting_provenance.title: Deterrent Effects of Essential Oils on Spotted-Wing Drosophila: Implications for Organic Management in Berry Crops
supporting_provenance.locator: Table 1; Methods 2.3; Results 3.2; Tables 2 and 3

evidence.finding: Methyl salicylate was one of eight broccoli volatiles that elicited antennal responses in diamondback moth; field trapping supported a three-component attractant blend.
limitations: Antennal response and blend attraction are not repellency, and the study did not prove methyl salicylate alone caused field attraction or oviposition.

supporting_provenance.title: Electrophysiological Responses and Field Attractants of Plutella xylostella Adults to Volatiles from Brassica oleracea
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract and Results: eight antennally active broccoli volatiles and field trapping with a three-component blend

evidence.finding: p-Cymene was one of five thyme monoterpenes reported to repel Culex pipiens pallens in a human forearm bioassay.
limitations: The abstract does not establish p-cymene as the strongest compound, and a later Culex study reported almost no p-cymene activity.

supporting_provenance.title: Monoterpenes from thyme as potential mosquito repellents
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: five monoterpenes and human forearm repellency against Culex pipiens pallens

evidence.finding: p-Cymene showed almost no repellent activity in the adult assay even though the two p-cymene-rich Thymus essential oils showed 72.9% and 78.1% repellency.
limitations: This conflicts with another Culex forearm study and shows that whole-oil activity cannot be assigned automatically to its dominant constituent.

supporting_provenance.title: Chemical composition, larvicidal evaluation, and adult repellency of endemic Greek Thymus essential oils against the mosquito vector of West Nile virus
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: Thymus oil repellency percentages and almost no p-cymene repellent activity

evidence.finding: Peppermint-oil laminate flakes reduced strawberry larval infestation by 25% four days after application, but not seven days after application.
limitations: The study did not isolate non-contact avoidance from contact, toxicity, egg laying, hatch, or larval survival.

supporting_provenance.title: Reduced Drosophila suzukii Infestation in Berries Using Deterrent Compounds and Laminate Polymer Flakes
supporting_provenance.locator: Results: strawberry laminate-flake field infestation at four and seven days after application

evidence.finding: Rosemary oil was a 10% component of Ecotrol PLUS, a rosemary/geraniol/peppermint mixture that reduced raspberry sentinel-fruit infestation versus an unsprayed control.
limitations: The study did not isolate rosemary oil's contribution and did not establish the efficacy of pure rosemary oil against SWD.

supporting_provenance.title: Deterrent Effects of Essential Oils on Spotted-Wing Drosophila: Implications for Organic Management in Berry Crops
supporting_provenance.locator: Table 1; Methods 2.3; Results 3.2; Tables 2 and 3

evidence.scientific_name: Aedes aegypti; Anopheles dirus; Culex quinquefasciatus
evidence.finding: Undiluted rosemary oil was tested directly by arm-in-cage against Aedes aegypti, Anopheles dirus, and Culex quinquefasciatus; the paper reports repellency attributes across the tested oils and species.
limitations: Protection time depended strongly on oil and mosquito species; the abstract does not identify rosemary as the best oil or support transfer to a branded or diluted formulation.

supporting_provenance.title: Effectiveness of Herbal Essential Oils as Single and Combined Repellents against Aedes aegypti, Anopheles dirus and Culex quinquefasciatus
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract and Methods: ten undiluted oils, including rosemary, tested by arm-in-cage against three mosquito species

evidence.endpoint: number blood-fed, human landings, and 24-hour mortality
evidence.finding: Over 12 months in Tanzanian experimental huts, two Guardian passive emanators containing 2,500 mg transfluthrin each reduced the number of blood-fed wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles arabiensis by 82.7% (95% CI 78.5%-86.1%), reduced human landings by 65.1% (95% CI 59.4%-70.0%), and induced 20.1% mortality at 24 hours (95% CI 18.4%-21.8%).
limitations: These are separate entomological endpoints from one product, deployment, experimental-hut design, and local vector population; the percentages cannot be added and do not estimate malaria-case reduction.

The fixed Guardian treatment assignment and volunteer-rotation protocol estimate performance under the reported hut conditions, not community coverage, adherence, diversion, malaria infection, clinical disease, or universal field efficacy.

The article contains contradictory declarations: its Funding section says no financial support was received, while its Conflict of interest section says the study received funding from SC Johnson and that the funder discussed data requirements and edited the manuscript. The Conflict of interest section also says the funder was not involved in study design, collection, analysis, interpretation, or the submission decision.

supporting_provenance.title: SC Johnson Guardian™ spatial repellent shows 1-year efficacy against wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles arabiensis, with a similar blood-feeding inhibition efficacy to Mosquito Shield™ in a Tanzanian experimental hut trial
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract Results; Materials and methods: Intervention, Study design, and endpoints; Results: Protective efficacy of Guardian; Tables 1-2: 12-month blood-feeding, landing, and 24-hour mortality estimates for wild pyrethroid-resistant Anopheles arabiensis; Statements: Funding and Conflict of interest

evidence.endpoint: first-time and overall new human malaria infections
evidence.finding: In the final analysis of a prospective, masked, cluster-randomized, placebo-controlled trial in 58 clusters in western Kenya after mass distribution of insecticide-treated nets, Mosquito Shield reduced the hazard of first-time malaria infection by 32.7% (95% two-sided CI 12.6%-48.2%; two-sided P=0.0030) and overall new malaria infections by 29.5% (95% CI 12.0%-43.5%; P=0.0020) among children 6 months to under 10 years old. Mosquito Shield and its matched placebo were allocated to 29 clusters per arm, deployed at two units per 9 square metres, and replaced every 28 days within a two-day window. The earlier 33.4% first-infection and 32.1% overall-infection estimates were interim results, not the final estimates.
limitations: This trial directly measured malaria infection in one high-transmission, pyrethroid-resistant setting with high insecticide-treated-net coverage; it does not make another product's hut endpoints a one-to-one disease-effect estimate.
The final first-infection result is reported in Results and Table 2; Figure 3 visualizes first-time infection incidence and does not report the overall-new-infection endpoint.
A different product cannot inherit Mosquito Shield's numerical infection reduction from shared active ingredient or superior hut performance.

supporting_provenance.title: Effect of a spatial repellent on malaria incidence in an area of western Kenya characterised by high malaria transmission, insecticide resistance, and universal coverage of insecticide treated nets (part of the AEGIS Consortium): a cluster-randomised, controlled trial
supporting_provenance.locator: Methods: Randomisation and masking and paragraph 22 intervention details; Results paragraphs 46-48 and Table 2: interim 33.4% first-time and 32.1% overall estimates distinguished from final 32.7% first-time and 29.5% overall estimates; Figure 3: first-time infection incidence only

evidence.endpoint: recommended use context and evidence certainty
evidence.finding: On 13 August 2025, WHO issued a conditional recommendation for indoor spatial emanators in addition to insecticide-treated nets to prevent and control malaria in children and adults in areas with ongoing malaria transmission. WHO described the recommendation as based on moderate-certainty evidence from five studies of passive emanators and mosquito coils.
limitations: The recommendation does not establish an exact numerical disease reduction for every product or setting.
WHO identified evidence gaps for spatial emanators used alone, outdoors, in humanitarian emergencies, and for insecticide-resistance management.

supporting_provenance.title: WHO recommends spatial emanators for malaria vector control and prequalifies first two products
supporting_provenance.locator: Departmental update dated 13 August 2025, paragraphs under 'More on the WHO recommendation': conditional indoor use in addition to ITNs, children and adults, ongoing malaria transmission, moderate-certainty evidence from five studies, and remaining evidence gaps

evidence.scientific_name: Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex mosquito vectors
evidence.finding: WHO lists SC Johnson Guardian as active prequalified product P-12643 from 13 August 2025. The product type is spatial emanator, the active ingredient is transfluthrin, and the listed concentration is 2.5 g per unit.
limitations: Prequalification is a candidate-product assessment of quality, safety, and efficacy; it does not make another product equivalent or assign Guardian a numerical malaria-incidence effect.

supporting_provenance.title: SC Johnson Guardian
supporting_provenance.locator: Product Identification and Prequalification Status: spatial emanator, reference P-12643, SC Johnson, transfluthrin 2.5 g/unit, active status, and 13 August 2025 prequalification date

evidence.scientific_name: Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex mosquito vectors
evidence.endpoint: evidence relied on for Guardian prequalification
evidence.finding: WHO's Guardian efficacy assessment relied on Guardian-specific free-flight-room studies and semi-field studies against wild free-flying Anopheles in Tanzania and Benin. Table 7 lists comparative study BIT084 NI, 'Comparative efficacy of Guardian to Mosquito Shield', under studies not used to inform decision making; WHO stated that it was not necessary to the decision, although it was assessed as reliably conducted.
limitations: The WHO efficacy assessment supports Guardian's product-specific prequalification. It does not treat superior comparative hut performance as a substitute for the candidate-specific dossier or as direct clinical proof.

supporting_provenance.title: WHO Public Assessment Report: WHOPAR Part 5, SC Johnson Guardian, Efficacy Assessment
supporting_provenance.locator: Section 3, Semi-field studies; Section 4, Efficacy conclusions; Table 7 on page 17: BIO016a-23, BIO096-22, BIT084 WP3, and 24-04-B/GLP relied on for decision making, while BIT084 NI comparative Guardian-to-Mosquito-Shield study was not used to inform decision making

evidence.scientific_name: Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex mosquito vectors
evidence.finding: WHO's August 2025 interim guidance states that dossiers for spatial emanator products claiming equivalence to a currently prequalified product are not accepted by the Vector Control Product Assessment team.
limitations: The guidance describes WHO prequalification dossier requirements. It is not itself a malaria-control recommendation or an epidemiological efficacy result.

supporting_provenance.title: Interim guidance for the prequalification assessment of spatial emanator products
supporting_provenance.locator: Section 9, page 20: 'Claiming equivalency to an already prequalified product' and statement that equivalence-claiming dossiers are not accepted

evidence.scientific_name: Aedes, Anopheles, and Culex mosquito vectors
evidence.finding: WHO Module 5 requires at least one candidate-product free-flight-room study using insecticide-susceptible and insecticide-resistant strains of Aedes, Culex, and Anopheles, one susceptible and one resistant strain for each of the three genera, and at least three semi-field studies against wild free-flying vector populations. Semi-field batches must have full Module 3 characterization.
limitations: These are minimum dossier requirements, not a guarantee of prequalification or a numerical disease-effect claim.

supporting_provenance.title: Spatial emanators guidance: Module 5 data requirements table
supporting_provenance.locator: Data requirements 5.1 and 5.2, pages 1-2: susceptible and resistant Aedes, Culex, and Anopheles free-flight-room strains; minimum three semi-field studies; Module 3 batch characterization

evidence.endpoint: human landing, household density, blood feeding, and human blood index
evidence.finding: In a 24-week crossover among 90 Tanzanian households, three outdoor coils containing 0.03% transfluthrin reduced Anopheles arabiensis human landings by approximately 80% (IRR 0.22, 95% CI 0.052-0.91; P=0.037). In the 80% incomplete-coverage condition, six non-user households were randomly selected among 30 households in each village rather than placed specifically at a treatment boundary. Their An. arabiensis human blood index was 0.30 versus 0.20 under no coverage (odds ratio 1.71, 95% CI 1.04-2.83; P=0.03), which the authors interpreted as slight diversion of human blood meals. Resting and blood-fed An. arabiensis density did not increase at non-user households. For An. funestus, the coils did not significantly reduce human landings or change human blood index, and no differential diversion to non-users was demonstrated. Resting An. funestus density was significantly higher in every intervention scenario than under no coverage; blood-fed density was significantly higher among users under complete and incomplete coverage.
limitations: This result concerns burned 0.03% transfluthrin coils, local outdoor-biting populations, and the study's specific coverage patterns; it does not establish the same diversion magnitude for other formulations, species, resistance states, places, or coverage levels.
The Anopheles arabiensis diversion signal was a change in human blood index, not an increase in total resting or blood-fed mosquito density at non-user households.

The six non-user households in each incomplete-coverage village-period were randomly selected; the study did not specifically test redistribution across an external treatment boundary or by distance from treated households.

Reduced landings at treated households measured personal protection, while community protection required outcomes at both users and non-users; the study did not measure malaria infection or clinical disease endpoints.

The study did not measure local susceptibility to transfluthrin, so it could not distinguish pyrethroid resistance from intrinsic species-specific response as an explanation for the Anopheles funestus result.

supporting_provenance.title: A crossover study to evaluate the diversion of malaria vectors in a community with incomplete coverage of spatial repellents in the Kilombero Valley, Tanzania
supporting_provenance.locator: Methods: Study design and Human landing catches; Results: Assessment of treatment efficacy and Resting collections and diversion; Tables 2 and 4-6: 24-week crossover among 90 households, 80% incomplete coverage with six randomly selected non-users per 30-household village-period, 0.03% transfluthrin landing, human blood index, resting-density, and blood-fed-density outcomes for Anopheles arabiensis and Anopheles funestus

evidence.finding: Thymol was the strongest laboratory repellent for up to 24 hours and reduced raspberry landings and larval infestation; in a no-choice assay it reduced landings 60% and infestation 50%.
limitations: Thymol also increased mortality relative to controls, so reduced infestation cannot be interpreted as pure non-toxic repellency.

supporting_provenance.title: Reduced Drosophila suzukii Infestation in Berries Using Deterrent Compounds and Laminate Polymer Flakes
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract and Results: laboratory screen, cotton-wick raspberry assays, landing, infestation, and mortality

evidence.finding: Thymol showed repellency similar to DEET in the reported human forearm bioassay against Culex pipiens pallens.
limitations: This is one Culex assay and does not establish equivalent protection across dose, time, formulation, or mosquito species.

supporting_provenance.title: Monoterpenes from thyme as potential mosquito repellents
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: thymol versus DEET in human forearm repellency against Culex pipiens pallens

evidence.finding: Two species-specific Thymus essential oils provided 72.9% and 78.1% repellency, statistically equal to icaridin in the reported assay.
limitations: Thyme oils vary by plant species and chemotype; these results do not establish the performance of generic or branded Thymus vulgaris oil.

supporting_provenance.title: Chemical composition, larvicidal evaluation, and adult repellency of endemic Greek Thymus essential oils against the mosquito vector of West Nile virus
supporting_provenance.locator: Abstract: Thymus leucospermus and Thymus teucrioides essential-oil adult repellency
